item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please tell me I'm crazy, but it seems the password box does not seem to be responding to anything. Any ideas as to why that is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are there any plans to complete this ever? It's been a while, but the author is still active on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(I was rather angry when I wrote the prior version of this comment, here's a nicer version)

Things Which Sparked Joy:
- The concept
- The balls to make something like this.

Things Which Did Not Spark Joy:
- A lot of the puzzles were not my cup of tea, feeling like arbitrary riddles that you have to get lucky with to solve rather than a solution that feels logically derived from clues. The one exception being parts of the image puzzle, as it feels like a real problem that would occur in-universe ands its clear what you need to do and how the solution derives from the setup. That's just me, I think other people like that kind of riddle-y puzzle.
- That the article is unfinished yet posted to the mainlist.
- That someone could find this article, spend satan-knows how many hours of their life solving it themselves, only to reach the last puzzle and waste countless hours of their life on it, not knowing it has no solution. This Discuss section is an optional, out-of-universe thing that a reader is not required or expected to read alongside the main article.

As for the last point, publishing incomplete masterpiece is not necessarily a bad thing, but there should be a clear in-work indication that it is incomplete for the reader's sake. In the example of the RPG The Awakening (https://www.newgrounds.com/portal/view/647411), the author put an unavoidable unmissable self-insert avatar the end explicitly telling the player that their is no game beyond that point and he has no plans to finish it.

- 1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my opinion what happened was the author ran out of steam. Plain and simple. They didn't want something they spent a lot of time on going to waste, so they posted it in its unfinished state. A skip that was doomed to never be finished from the moment it was posted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
look through the recent posts for my opinion. i second everyone else: this is blatantly unfinished work, and the reader has to play through puzzles to get ANY information on the article whatsoever.

i was going to downvote it on the puzzle aspect alone — ambitious at best, frustrating and out of place at worst — but knowing the author has skipped out on the wiki cements it. -1. hopefully more people switch their votes to "down" from "up" over the next few months.

item.title: Lost Potential
item.content:encoded: I really feel like this SCP had potential, but then the author just had to leave it unfinished. Unfinished work shouldn't be left up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I completely agree with this statement. I worked for hours on these puzzles, only to be let down. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
seconding all of the "either have someone finish it or get rid of it" comments. There's no place for literal unfinished work on the wiki, no matter how ambitious it is.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I came late to the party, so I don't have the same right to be disappointed by the lack of an ending as the people who came in when this was first posted. I'm not going to downvote this, because it isn't a bad article, but I won't upvote it either. This just makes me sad, and slightly concerned for the author's well being. I hope you're doing well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finish your SCP and I will remove my Downvote. I love the idea of a puzzle SCP, but as it stands this is an unfinished article, which should not have been uploaded if you're unwilling to complete it.

item.title: Can Someone Add an Ending?
item.content:encoded:
I personally messaged the author of this article ages ago about finishing it and haven't heard back. I don't know what their opinions on this article are but I think if its going to be finished it needs to be done externally. As an avid puzzle solver, I love this article, but I think even if you're not huge on them you have to admit a lot of love and effort went into this scp, and it seems a waste to just delete it or something.

For what its worth:
+ My take on the intended twist of this scp
- This is my best guess

It looks like the intended twist is that this scp distracts and bamboozles people with puzzles it develops, causing them to become absent-minded and obsessed, and potentially providing it with opportunities to escape or perform other shenanigans. This would *usually* be a boring case of scp-that-causes-arbitrary-obsession-until-someone-is-harmed, but the beauty of this article is, if this is the intended twist, you have to obsess over the puzzles yourself in order to fully understand this idea. In other words, understanding this scp's ability requires falling prone to it in a very real way. That seems pretty fantastic to me.

If that were the case, I'd expect the finale to be some sort of incredibly unsatisfying-ly easy puzzle which unveils some document about the scp, with maybe the first letter of each line spelling 'This isn't the final puzzle'. Hence, you'd send the reader on an obsessive combing of the article to try and find the true final puzzle when there is none - they've just fallen prone to the obsession the scp causes.

In short, this seems like an article with a chance to do something no other scp has: literally cause the reader to become obsessed with it in the same way as you would in-universe (for an admittedly limited audience), and therefore seems like a shame to just discard. Is it possible for admins to set someone up with providing this article with much needed closure?

item.title: imma be real chief
item.content:encoded:
is there anywhere to just
find what it is
no one wants to actually solve this
edit: apparently its still not even finished yet pfft
nice one
Still no conclusion after four years. I love this, but I'm down voting strictly on principle.

item.title: General Info
item.content:encoded: I found that Anaxagoras was on this site up until June 24, 2018. They never had any plans of redoing this skip, I think this because I looked through every single edit on the sandbox and none of the activity showed any signs of the final ending. He also "forgot" to put this skip on his author page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this the way that this SCP has been done. There hasn't been anything else like it on the site.

Unfortunately, I feel that I have to downvote it. It has been up for many years and the final part is still not done. Effectively, it ends on a cliffhanger that has never been resolved, despite the author saying that it would be.

Again, downvote only due to the article's incomplete nature, not due to the article's content or execution.

item.title: Re: Oh.
item.content:encoded: The author states that an answer was close in 2015. He probably hasn't been on this site in years and we'll never get an answer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is [REDACTED]

item.title: Oh.
item.content:encoded:
So, this is actually the last article on my Great Series III Readthrough of Ultimate Destiny™, and it was a pretty spectacular way to end it off.

The puzzles made me want to go to the comments for an answer more than once (ha i sukks at puzzels topkek), which both hurt my pride as a self-proclaimed Puzzlemaster and made me grin like a nimrod at the absolute beauty of this SCP's complexity. Good job, Anax.

That said, when it was all said and done, and I had read all there was to read, I learned that there was initially supposed to be an ending of some sort to this oversized meta deathtrap… which I would have liked, as the article felt very open-ended to me. Then, I looked at the creation date of this article. 2015. Three years ago. Three years without an ending.

If there's one thing you never, ever, ever do, it's interest me, then refuse me the closure I so desperately want.

This is a downvote for now, but I will keep tabs on this article for future developments, however.

item.title: Re: Not working?
item.content:encoded: You're probably blocking third-party cookies. For some reason, that doesn't just break the tracking I use for the puzzles, it also breaks the Javascript I use to display dynamic content.

item.title: Re: Have People Been Missing the Story Here?
item.content:encoded: You're surprisingly close to the ending I have in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final password should at least confirm our suspicions that it was all a trap.

item.title: I sent message to creater
item.content:encoded: But when he updates colored text?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there any in-universe explanation for the puzzles? I feel like if there was a containment breach, everyone would die because they had to get past 5 puzzles. Is there even an ending?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the password for the [REDACTED]?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't want this to be deleted, but I would like someone to add the missing payoff for the final puzzle. Someone other than the original author, if need be.

item.title: Such a Waste...
item.content:encoded:
I mean, so much obvious time and effort went into this and it was so well-received… yet, it's never been completed. Unless I'm missing something, the final password never goes through meaning the big payoff is denied and that's just the absolute worst feeling after spending all that time on those puzzles. I have to downvote it on that issue alone.

We're closing in on 3 years without a finish…

item.title: Re: Not working?
item.content:encoded: The author never finished this.

item.title: Not working?
item.content:encoded:
I have a couple of correct passwords (I solved the octagon one, albeit with some help for the 'extraction' part from the discussion page.) I can enter them into the box and "Check", but don't get an incorrect password message. This is what the page looks like for me:

Screenshot
Is this what it's supposed to look like?
]

Of course, I could just be stupid and missing any changes that happen when I enter in a password.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Closed
item.content:encoded:
The sentiment is appreciated, but that said, please don't call other site members assholes.

I'm calling a stop to this line of discussion. Please keep further conversation focused on the article itself from now on.

Do not reply if you are not a staff member. This is a closed staff post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right, Zyn's polite and subtle approach doesn't seem to be working, so I'll be blunt: stop being an asshole or you won't last long on this site (Zyn is an admin, FYI).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll lose a lot of sleep over that one…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's fine if you're not interested in certain formats for SCP articles.

However, calling things you don't like "crap" just because you don't like them reflects badly on you as a reader, and makes it less likely that community members will want to interact with you in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not here to play crappy puzzle games. If I could, I'd downvote it again for the gimmick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Hell is this crap?

An interactive puzzle SCP.

Even 579 isn't this much of a [DATA EXPUNGED] happy waste of an article.

…probably because 579 isn't an interactive puzzle SCP.

ETA: actually, given the tone of the above post, let me explain further so it's clearer. This is a puzzle SCP article. The links to other pages present puzzles. Solving the puzzles gives you passwords. Entering the passwords into the box at the top of this article will reveal parts of the article's main text.

If you don't want to solve the puzzles, there's a couple posts floating around in the discussion that have the answers/passwords. You can use those for the unlocks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Hell is this crap? Even 579 isn't this much of a [DATA EXPUNGED] happy waste of an article.

Downvoted.

item.title: Re: If you won't mind...
item.content:encoded: Actually, it's rot-12, 19, 0, 22, 7, 23, 13. What you've translated as "De?" is actually "No?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article is interesting, I don't really care much for puzzles (I really don't like them) but the idea of the format screw itself is commendable. However, I will change my upvote to a downvote seeing as there is no payoff.

item.title: what are the umbers for
item.content:encoded: just completed the puzzle and there are coloured numbers at the top of the page and the colours match the redacted so what are they about ???

item.title: Have People Been Missing the Story Here?
item.content:encoded:
Now, to be fair, I love puzzles, so that didn't put me off this SCP. But I think what people don't realise is what the real story behind this SCP is (or, at least, what I can make of it).

Handwavy Speculation
Hide Speculation

On the surface, this SCP does seem quite mundane. Made of plasma, causes compulsions in humans, nothing special. But (and I think this may have been explored more in the ending we never got), it seems to me like the 'compelling people to speak with it.' component of this SCP - especially given its interview log - is that it causes people to obsess over complex puzzles.

Now this in itself isn't particularly special either, but the entire article takes advantage of this fact. The SCP has corrupted the entire article into several difficult puzzles that encourage you to obsess over them. By solving - and hence reading - this article, you inevitably become a victim of SCP-2212. You've fallen into its trap by obsessing over the puzzles its provided you with. You could even say its the desire for hints, clues or nudges in the right direction that let this SCP cause so many breaches - guards become obsessed with the SCP's puzzles, and push themselves to ask for clues, or even just additional puzzles.

Now it would be unfair to say that the article is genius because of this, given that the explanation I've given here is largely implied and could be totally wrong, but at the same time I think people need to reconsider what this SCP is about. People who hate puzzles find an underwhelming SCP underneath because they haven't experienced the slow realisation that you've become just another researcher under the influence of SCP-2212 - the story is underwhelming because without that sense of curiosity, obsession, realisation and dread, this article is largely mundane.

In other words, unless you're a fan of universal critical acclaim and think all SCP articles should be enjoyable at maximum capacity by everyone, then you should value the fact that this article chooses its target audience and absolutely blows them away. You don't have to personally feel the rush that everyone else does to appreciate the fact that it exists, and has been crafted beautifully (despite the flaws and red herrings present in some puzzles).

Hope the author decides to finish this article one day, because it could easily be my favourite SCP with the right ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upon reflection, I have downvoted this for never being completed by the author. This was an incredibly ambitious concept and I am supremely disappointed that it was left half-done.

item.title: If you won't mind...
item.content:encoded:
…I'll take my article without Cipher BS, thank you.

+ YA WANKAHS
- Because It's Australian.

Whatever text to show/hide.

* Item #: SCP-2212

Object Class: Euclid

Special Containment Procedures:
Revision 21

SCP-2212 is to be contained in a cylindrical titanium chamber, 3 meters in radius by 2 meters in height. The chamber is to be lined with lead, and illuminated at all times. As SCP-2212 does not require sustenance, no personnel are to access it except for testing.

Outside this, an additional secondary containment chamber is to be constructed, with a radius of 30 meters. This chamber is to be octagonal, and radially subdivided into 8 chambers, facing in the cardinal and intercardinal directions.

Should personnel need to interact with SCP-2212, it is strongly recommended that they keep their distance and keep radiation-resistant barriers between them and SCP-2212 at all times. Contact with SCP-2212, however brief, during periods of elevated radiance is prohibited.

Recent audits of security footage have found that SCP-2212’s periodic containment breaches have been caused by partially compromised guards releasing it. Transfer of the project to a new team and upgrade to Keter status is pending. In the interim period, it is believed that SCP-2212’s containment cell alone should be enough to restrain it. New containment procedures will be developed based on further audit results.

Description: SCP-2212 is a loosely humanoid entity of unknown, apparently inorganic composition. It is animate and mobile, though shows no signs of physical aggression. It has two arms and a head, though lacks legs, and instead moves through hopping on its elongated trunk. Efforts to take samples of its material have yielded inconclusive results, most typically suggesting that SCP-2212 is made of some sort of plasma. SCP-2212 stands about 1.9 meters tall, and weighs about 35 kg.

SCP-2212 also emits moderate and variable quantities of light at various points throughout the spectrum, including as gamma ray. Measurements indicate that a person standing one meter away from SCP-2212 for one minute would typically be exposed to about 0.3 Sv, enough to cause mild radiation poisoning and long-term increase of cancer risk. Radiation output tends to decrease when no persons are interacting with it, but also sporadically increases as much as tenfold. No clear pattern for this has yet been found.

SCP-2212 is sapient and highly intelligent. It is capable of speaking and understanding most common languages, and learns new ones rapidly. SCP-2212 claims to be unable to [REDACTED] information regarding its nature.

Persons interacting with or studying SCP-2212 may be subject to deleterious mental effects, including amnesia, minor bouts of [REDACTED], and in rare cases, having their behavior directly influenced by SCP-2212. This influence has so far primarily taken the form of compelling people to speak with it. Notably, this has most often coincided with periods of increased radiation from SCP-2212, though affected personnel will still don safety gear prior to approaching SCP-2212. Affected personnel report feeling as though they were in a [REDACTED] state, aware of their actions, but calm and distant from them.

SCP-2212 was recovered after Foundation monitoring stations in Australia detected reality disturbances consistent with interdimensional travel. As there are many unmonitored Ways in central Australia, this would not itself have warranted extensive investigation save that mild seismic activity was measured coincident with those observations. Agents were dispatched to investigate the center of the disturbance. They reported that SCP-2212 did not resist the agents’ efforts to transport it to Site 260, and appeared to recognize the Foundation [REDACTED] on their equipment. However, all four agents were [REDACTED] due to SCP-2212’s radiation. A second expedition was able to safely contain SCP-2212, which was later transported to Site 17 for a more secure environment.

Interview 2212-104:

Dr. Mueller: Hello, SCP-2212. I’d like to ask you a few questions about your background and goals. Firstly, the October 18 event where we recovered you from. Our sensors detected major reality disruptions consistent with cross-dimensional travel without the assistance of a Way or some other stable path, but there was also seismic activity that our best research suggests should have nothing to do with that. What produced that?

SCP-2212: Junior researcher, reading scripts. They are not trying very hard to learn about me, are they? Still, it will be enough. And even from seeds like you can sprout wondrous ideas. Your researchers are correct; the path between worlds does not require shifting the matter of the worlds themselves. But the earthquake was not unrelated. Figure it out.

Dr. Mueller: Ah, I don’t think I will be the one to work on that. We’ve learned enough about what you do.

SCP-2212: You have not. For instance, right now, you are wondering about my word choice. You have read all interviews with me, and know that my syntax can be entirely normal. But to do so would make matters far too simple.

Dr. Mueller: And you’d never want that. Hm, let’s see. Since being taken into Foundation custody, you have precipitated no fewer than three containment breach events. Yet despite on several occasions being able to exit the facility while the guards are incapacitated, you stayed. Why?

SCP-2212: Why would I want to leave the Foundation? True, it’s hard for me to exit this chamber without first having to cause a breach, but I don’t mind that. You all are such excellent scientists, and I love how you never stop digging. I could grow quite fat and happy in a place like that.

Dr. Mueller: There! You talked normally. I’m not sure how; it’s more than just using “I” again, but I can’t quite put my finger on it. No, stop that. It won’t get you anywhere.

SCP-2212: De?

Dr. Mueller: Dr. Ricardo? I’m terminating the interview. This isn’t working. Try someone who doesn’t understand what they’re reading, maybe.

SCP-2212: That will be enough to have you, Sonia Mueller. You will be freed, do not worry. But before you go, what do you understand about my matter of speaking in this interview?

Dr. Mueller: If I listen to the sounds, you’re gibbering. I can’t even produce those sounds. But I understand you, which means that you’re in my head and have been there since I walked into the room. That must be how you did that to those guards in your last breach. Shit, we can’t talk to you at all, can we? Okay, you weren’t using “I” and I don’t think you used “a”. Is this some sort of letter substitution? But you sounded so different each time, and you spoke normally once—

Dr. Ricardo: Dr. Mueller, are you okay? Exit the room.

SCP-2212: She will exit shortly. Very good, Dr. Mueller. You comprehend the essence of it, but your employers would likely kill you if you lingered to figure out more. Which would be no good for either of us.

Dr. Mueller: Luis, I’m out. Repeat, terminate the interview. I do not want to ever interact with that fucking thing again. How isn’t it Keter?

SCP-2212: Doctors, one last word before you turn off your recordings: “REALISE”. There are seven things from me that will go into your logs today. From each, you may extract the key to each of the seven letters in my final message to you today. Simply see what “a” would go to, and apply the same transformation to the corresponding letter. Think deep, and the truth will come to you.

Cross-Tests:

Experiment 2212-1220
Date: September 5, 2016
Head Researchers: Dr. Luis Ricardo


Using rot12, 19, 12, 7, and 13.
The code for the image is daydreamlike.

item.title: Re: Is it done?
item.content:encoded: Will it ever be added?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just came here looking for the whole Appolyon thing and got distracted by a Puzzle SCP for a good two hours. THAT is the signature of a good SCP.
Congrats, my sir, very nice. I Dr.Nichts approves.

item.title: Re: Is it done?
item.content:encoded: no

item.title: Is it done?
item.content:encoded: So has the final part been added?

item.title: answers
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

The answer is
DAYDREAMLIKE
LOGOS
LOST
SYNCOPE
REMEMBER


So yeah!
spoiler alert btw

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Until the finale gets added, I'll keep my vote neutral.

Why I would downvote: I see spelling and grammar errors here and there, unless that's the intention.

Why I would upvote: It's a FREAKING PUZZLE SCP!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The mainlist has SCP articles, which are usually largely about anomalies. This isn't a necessity though. Similarly, modern articles usually tell a story, but this isn't an absolute requirement. Sometimes it's just a cool thing, either conceptually or mechanically. I appreciate that this isn't working for you, but I think it's a mistake to try and say what does or doesn't belong on the site beyond what people are or aren't willing to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still, the mainlist showcases SCPs and how to contain them and how they came to be. The articles tell a story. The puzzles are unique but as an article is not worthwhile. There is hardly any story or the puzzles contribute much to that story. The puzzles could be posted elsewhere on-site for those to have fun with the puzzles solely than end up with an underwhelming concept than they were hoping. SCP themed puzzles are kinda interesting but used to build up something not, again, worthwhile here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would argue that the puzzles are the point. They clearly aren't working for you, but the anomaly doesn't have to be the focal point of every article. If this was the same thing but "written normally," I would argue that it would be a significantly weaker piece in comparison in that it doesn't bring anything particularly new to the table aside from its core feature (the puzzles.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's very convoluted and has such a short line of appeal. The main page of this looks devoid of content (either because it is) and the puzzles don't seem to say much. What I can tell is that this SCP is some humanoid thing made of plasma that affects reality when it travels but covered in useless puzzles along with compulsive and radioactive traits. It makes this article just very discouraging and not worthwhile to new readers too. Also, from an in-universe perspective, everyone will die if they can't access the procedures due to a damn puzzle for an SCP that isn't even worth having one for.

Still, this whole thing could have been written normally. Was it really necessary to have to solve a puzzle for the damn object class? If it's under layers of stuff then it's clearly in some way a threat. This format screw was just a headache, I recommend readers to just read the page source. Also, the puzzle supposedly isn't even finish so that's another thing why you should all read the page source.

Overall, a weak, incomplete, format screw that is convoluted and unnecessary towards it's underwhelming anomaly. Downvoted -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I solved the whole thing in one pass. It was quite fun, especially in the cross-testing section. I liked the way you used other SCPs as part of the puzzle, that was quite clever.

Having done the MIT Mystery Hunt, the puzzles definitely had a similar feel, though obviously significantly easier.

As for the fact that it is currently unfinished, this is true but doesn't detract (much) from the experience. The only thing that I wish could be added is an indication that the final meta-answer is correct and no further information is available.

Something like a popup saying:

"Scanning …
Scanning …
All available information is either fully decoded or permanently lost."

The slap a "Data unrecoverable" on the cross-testing logs and perhaps an addendum, and it's complete, with no new material.

It's a much better option that deleting it, if it comes to that. Keeping it as is works as well.

item.title: sigh....
item.content:encoded: I admitted…. I use this article as a "spare-time game"

item.title: No ending, but...
item.content:encoded: I hope the final password will do something though.

item.title: Re: Did some digging around the source code for SCP-2212:
item.content:encoded: And if you notice, there is no check for the final password. This is still incomplete after >2 years. Something's gotta be done.

item.title: Did some digging around the source code for SCP-2212:
item.content:encoded:
And found this

var imagePassword = -1705883138;
var containmentPassword = -522328435;
var interviewPassword = -1742484951;
var crossTestPassword = 103149608;
var classPassword = 3327780;
var finalPassword = 104069929

For a shorter version:

image Password = -1705883138
containmentPassword = -522328435
interviewPassword = -1742484951
crossTestPassword = 103149608
classPassword = 3327780
finalPassword = 104069929

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is actually nothing there

There is no final password

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can someone tell me what the password is so I can actually know what 2212 is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the few times I wish I had read the spoilers first. Reaching the "incorrect password" prompt is like playing a video game where you reach the end, opening what appears to be the gate leading to the final boss, and then suddenly the credits roll. The game may have been fun up to this point, but I'll be damned if I don't feel cheated anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just couldn't get into this article, it's hard to believe this is how the Foundation would handle data corruption in-universe.

To add to that, it's unfinished. There's no ending to this even after all the work readers put into finishing the puzzles.
